title: Corporate Profit Growth Returns
gist: Gavekal Dragonomics' Thomas Gatley reports China's corporate profits surged abruptly in early 2026 from the confluence of the global AI investment boom and windfall oil-and-gas earnings from the Iran war energy shock—but expects profits to cool in H2 as the oil windfall ends, while the AI-driven growth continues.

themes.theme: China Corporate Profit Cycle: AI Boom Plus Iran War Windfall

insights.claim: After little growth from 2023 to 2025 (2–3% per year), China's corporate profits surged abruptly in early 2026 driven by the global AI investment boom and windfall earnings in the oil and gas sector from the Iran war energy price shock.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: China's profit cycle has inflected sharply higher on a dual-driver basis (AI + energy windfall); this supports Chinese equity earnings revisions to the upside—but investors need to decompose the drivers since one (oil windfall) is transitory.
insights.quote: China's corporate profits have started off 2026 with an abrupt surge, thanks to a combination of the global investment boom in artificial intelligence and windfall earnings from the energy-price shock during the Iran war.

insights.claim: Chinese mainland and Hong Kong-listed industrial and services firms (~5,000 total) posted 40% revenue growth in 2021-22, but that pace slowed to just 2-3% per year from 2023 to 2025.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: The 2021-22 boom was a post-COVID demand surge that has since normalized; the new 2026 acceleration is structurally different (AI-driven capex + oil windfall), not a replay of the pandemic recovery.
insights.quote: mainland and Hong Kong-listed industrial and services-sector firms—numbering about 5,000 in total, excluding financial and real-estate companies—posted 40% revenue growth, while sales among the roughly half a million firms included in the official industrial survey rose 30%. But that pace slowed to a crawl from 2023 to 2025, rising by only about 2-3% per year.
insights.locator: Chinese companies enjoyed a phenomenal two-year run in 2021 and 2022

insights.claim: Gavekal expects China's profit growth to cool in H2 2026 as the oil-and-gas windfall ends, but still come in stronger for the full year; the AI boom is expected to continue while domestic demand remains weak.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: China corporate earnings have a clear H1/H2 bifurcation risk: overweight companies with AI exposure (durable driver) and underweight those whose 2026 earnings are oil-windfall-driven; the H2 deceleration is already telegraphed by Gavekal.
insights.quote: growth in China's corporate profits will cool off in the second half of the year, but will still come in stronger this year.
